elements.sent-2411-002: Rust aims to offer full memory safety for programs, a guarantee that untamed C programs do not enjoy.
elements.sent-2411-003: How difficult is it to translate existing C code to Rust? To get a complementary view from that of automatic C to Rust translators, we report on a user study asking humans to translate real-world C programs to Rust.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our participants are able to produce safe Rust translations, whereas state-of-the-art automatic tools are not able to do so.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our analysis highlights that the high-level strategy taken by users departs significantly from those of automatic tools we study.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also find that users often choose zero-cost (static) abstractions for temporal safety, which addresses a predominant component of runtime costs in other full memory safety defenses.
elements.sent-2411-007: User-provided translations showcase a rich landscape of specialized strategies to translate the same C program in different ways to safe Rust, which future automatic translators can consider.
elements.sent-2411-008: Memory safety vulnerabilities in C programs are still a prominent category of CVEs reported in commodity software, and number in thousands each year~\cite{ossfuzz .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Several approaches to secure such unsafe code are being investigated.
elements.sent-2411-010: Full memory safety through runtime checks inserted by compiler instrumentation is achievable.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, it incurs high performance overhead ($\geq 50\%$) and is often not deployed in production~\cite{cets,softbound .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Instead, partial memory safety defenses which have overheads below $20\%$ have found deployment~\cite{cfi2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Emerging hardware features can reduce performance overheads of partial safety techniques, but even then, faulty programs that will often ungracefully abort in production systems have limited appeal.
elements.sent-2411-014: Similarly, automatic patching techniques that can localize bugs and suggest fixes for them are being developed~\cite{faultloc,senx,gao2022programrepair,song2024provenfix .}
elements.sent-2411-015: But these approaches do not aim to rule out the existence of memory safety bugs altogether.
elements.sent-2411-016: A different approach has been to {\em write secure code which is free of memory safety bugs from the ground up.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The idea is to have a stricter language and compiler that forces developers to rewrite unsafe code to use safe patterns everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-018: The advantage is that full memory safety can be achieved {\em mostly statically, while a few runtime checks incur low overheads.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Designing safe C dialects with that goal has a long history.
elements.sent-2411-020: These dialects largely aim to retain low-level features of C for compatibility, making it difficult to move away from unsafe C patterns entirely while keeping overheads low~\cite{cyclone,necula2005ccured .}
elements.sent-2411-021: More recent designs aim for better compatibility by allowing mixed (statically) safe and unsafe C~\cite{checkedc , but when temporal memory safety is desired in them, overheads can exceed $30\%$~\cite{3c .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Rust is an alternative mainstream language that offers low-level control over memory, while providing full memory safety.
elements.sent-2411-023: It has a growing ecosystem and increasing support from commodity OSes.
elements.sent-2411-024: It departs from the conventional approaches to safe C dialects in that it largely abandons the programming abstractions in C, such as the use of unchecked raw pointers and unsafe type casts.
elements.sent-2411-025: It is natural to ask: How difficult is it to translate existing C code to Rust then? In the last few years, automatic techniques to translate C code to Rust have started to emerge.
elements.sent-2411-026: There are two main approaches, one based on compiler-based analysis~\cite{emre2021translating,c2rust and another based on large language models (LLMs)~\cite{eniser2024towards,yang2024vert .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: But both approaches have had very limited effectiveness so far, even on small programs of about $100$ lines of code.
elements.sent-2411-029: al. report that only about $11\%$ of raw C pointers can be converted to safe Rust references through static analysis~\cite{emre2021translating .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Similarly, recent work on \florine~\cite{eniser2024towards reports that less than half of the small C programs they consider can be auto-translated to Rust using LLM-based repair.}
elements.sent-2411-031: It is unclear what strategies, if any, enable a successful search for C to Rust translations.
elements.sent-2411-032: In order to gain a complementary perspective, in this paper, we study how {\em human users approach the C to Rust translation task .}
elements.sent-2411-033: We conducted a user study in which we asked undergraduate students taking a course in computer security to translate a given set of real-world C programs to safe Rust.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our participants are familiar with C and memory safety issues, but have minimal prior exposure to Rust.
elements.sent-2411-035: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first such analysis of a user study on C to Rust translation.
elements.sent-2411-036: Most of the users succeed in providing reasonable translations of our C benchmark to Rust, whereas state-of-the-art automatic C to Rust translation tools are ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-037: We highlight several high-level principles and strategies that are common across the user translations, which could be useful for future automatic translators.
elements.sent-2411-038: First, we find that all user-provided Rust translations {\em semantically lift from the low-level abstractions used in the C code to re-express the logic in Rust, rather than trying to mimic the original program and data-flow structure too closely.}
elements.sent-2411-039: This approach helps break free of the low-level constraints present in the original C code, which would violate the strict rules in Rust if preserved.
elements.sent-2411-040: Rust enforces stricter rules on pointer aliasing than C.
elements.sent-2411-041: Users decomposed the object lifetimes in different ways that are {\em specialized to access patterns used for the object to satisfy Rust rules.}
elements.sent-2411-042: This shows that there are multiple strategies to translate the same C program to safe Rust, with room for context-specific translation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-043: Second, we find that resulting Rust translations often contain zero-cost statically-checked safety abstractions.
elements.sent-2411-044: Temporal memory safety, which is a source of significant runtime overhead in many prior defenses~\cite{cets,3c , is achieved mostly statically.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The resulting Rust programs \todoadd{have comparable performance to C , and rarely more than $20\%$ slower than the corresponding C code, even though our participants are not explicitly asked to optimize for performance.}
elements.sent-2411-046: At the same time, known spatial and temporal vulnerabilities in our C benchmarks are eliminated in the translated Rust code.
elements.sent-2411-047: This suggests that C to Rust translation is worth it, as the trade-off between performance and security we observe in translations by non-expert Rust users is desirable.
elements.sent-2411-048: Third, nearly all Rust translations have functional discrepancies compared with the corresponding C code.
elements.sent-2411-049: These discrepancies are easy to find with fuzzing and Rust translations of the same C program have correlated failures, i.e., they often fail the same tests.
elements.sent-2411-050: But, several others are logical errors, suggesting a ``last mile'' phenomenon at play: While users find it feasible to convert most C functionality to its Rust counterpart, making a $100\%$ correct translation is tedious.
elements.sent-2411-051: Several findings from our user study, which involves non-experts, are also seen in the open-source project maintained by more experienced Rust developers.. Contribution.
elements.sent-2411-052: We report on the lessons garnered from the first user study on translating real-world C programs to Rust by non-expert Rust users.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our work sheds light on strategies and policy decisions that users have to make, which are complementary to automatic mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-054: We highlight the end gains in user-provided translations, such as the ubiquitous use of zero-cost temporal safety abstractions, \todoadd{elimination of unsafe code patterns, and the trade-offs with functional correctness.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Rust allows programs to have fine-grained control over memory but with spatial and temporal memory safety.
elements.sent-2411-056: We briefly explain Rust design principles for achieving full memory safety with a running example shown in Figure~\ref{fig:introrust .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[trim=0cm 1.74cm 4.76cm 0cm, clip, width=\ourfigwidth]{imgs/introrust0.pdf} \caption{A Rust code example showing the concepts of ownership transfer, mut./immut. borrowing, and reference lifetime.} \label{fig:introrust}
elements.sent-2411-057: Rust ensures that each data object has an exclusive {\em owner at any given program point~\cite{ownershipilya .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: When the variable owning an object goes out of scope, the compiler automatically deallocates the object.
elements.sent-2411-059: The single-owner principle ensures that the object is deallocated only once, avoiding double-free bugs prevalent in C programs.
elements.sent-2411-060: The compiler knows the scope of all objects in the program.
elements.sent-2411-061: Stack variables have statically determined block/function scope, and so do globals.
elements.sent-2411-062: Objects can be owners of other objects, creating a transitive chain of ownership.
elements.sent-2411-063: The chain starts with a stack/global variable, so the compiler can track ownership of all objects.
elements.sent-2411-065: Ownership can not be duplicated but only transferred between variables through assignment or parameter passing.
elements.sent-2411-066: The Rust compiler, therefore, tracks the unique owner of every object at every program point.
elements.sent-2411-067: For example, the \code{buf variable in the example of Figure~\ref{fig:introrust is on the stack and encapsulates a (smart) pointer to \code{Vec object allocated on the heap.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The object allocated on Line 2 in Figure~\ref{fig:introrust has a unique owner \code{buf from Line 2-14, after which the ownership transfers to the first parameter of \code{use_buf() .}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The object will be deallocated when the new owner goes out of scope, i.e., at the end of the function scope of \code{use_buf() .. Borrowing.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Exclusive ownership, by itself, disallows all aliasing altogether, which is too restrictive.
elements.sent-2411-071: Rust relaxes this restriction by introducing a {\em borrowing mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-072: It allows creating {\em references (also called {\em borrows ) to temporarily access the data value without transferring the ownership.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: References \code{a , \code{a1 , and \code{x borrow from the owner \code{buf , whereas \code{x0 {\em reborrows from \code{x in the example.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: This allows limited forms of aliasing.. Lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-075: With borrowed references, it becomes essential to ensure that a reference never points to an object that has been deallocated.
elements.sent-2411-076: The Rust compiler statically tracks the {\em lifetime of variables: the region of code that the variable must be valid for.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Rust enforces that the lifetime of all references is a strict subset of the lifetime of the owner statically.
elements.sent-2411-078: Since the object will be deallocated only when the lifetime of the owner expires, all live references point to valid objects, eliminating use-after-free vulnerabilities that arise in C programs.
elements.sent-2411-079: The example in Figure \ref{fig:introrust creates multiple references to an array object.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The variable \code{buf gets ownership of the heap-allocated array on Line 2.}
elements.sent-2411-081: There are four borrowed references created directly or indirectly from \code{buf on lines 3, 4, 9, and 11.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Their lifetimes are enforced to be smaller than that of \code{buf and are highlighted.}
elements.sent-2411-083: The object is automatically deallocated only when the lifetime of the owner \code{buf ends, thereby ensuring that all references point to valid memory throughout their lifetime.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Aliasing references can update the object storage, possibly moving them, and associated allocation metadata (e.g. size, internal pointers) can become inconsistent.
elements.sent-2411-086: When multiple references are pointing to the same object (e.g. a vector type) to insert/delete elements or change its capacity, the location of its internal buffer and size metadata might change.
elements.sent-2411-087: Consequently, this will invalidate other references to the same object, including iterators and references to its elements.
elements.sent-2411-088: Memory-unsafe languages like C leave careful management of aliased pointers \tododel{and their semantic metadata to the programmer, which has been a source of mistakes.}
elements.sent-2411-089: To avoid this, Rust proposes {\em Aliasing Xor Mutability (AXM) principle.}
elements.sent-2411-090: First, references are divided into two types: mutable (\code{ mut ) and immutable (\code{ )\tododel{, prefixed with \code{ mut and \code{ declarations keywords respectively in Rust .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Mutable references can read and write the referent object, while immutable ones have read-only access.
elements.sent-2411-092: Second, the Rust compiler enforces that either only one mutable reference or multiple (aliasing) immutable references are active at a program point.
elements.sent-2411-093: Mutable aliased references can not be active at the same program point.
elements.sent-2411-094: The AXM principle eliminates the need for careful pointer invalidation by the programmer and helps memory safety in concurrent code as well.
elements.sent-2411-095: Specifically in the example of Figure~\ref{fig:introrust , Lines 3-6 have multiple immutable references to \code{buf , and Lines 9-13 have one mutable reference at each point, created using \code{ mut type declaration.}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The compiler can statically infer the lifetime of \code{x and other mutable references that may alias it, such as \code{x0 .}}
elements.sent-2411-097: It automatically splits their range of legal use such that there will be a single mutable reference to the object active in scope at each statement, i.e, \code{x is permitted on Line $(9-10, 13)$ and \code{x0 on Lines 11-12.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: The compiler would forbid using \code{x0 on or after line 13 as it would create two mutable references to the vector elements, highlighted by commented code on Lines 14 for illustration.}
elements.sent-2411-099: In the equivalent C code, dereferencing \code {x0 on Line 14 would be legal and lead to undefined behavior, since \code {x0 refers to a vector whose underlying elements were cleared (possibly deallocated) via \code{x on Line 13.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: This can result in out-of-bound access due to a dangling dereference.
elements.sent-2411-101: Such memory errors are disallowed by the AXM principle in Rust.
elements.sent-2411-103: The AXM principle can be too restrictive in certain circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-104: When multiple mutable references are necessary, Rust programs can resort to runtime borrow checks and reference counting for memory safety, through the use of data types such as \code{RefCell<T> and \code{Rc<T> \todoadd{for single-thread synchronization and \code{Mutex<T> and \code{Arc<T> for multi-thread synchronization.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: These generic types are implemented in the Rust standard library with internal use of unsafe Rust and have been proven sound~\cite{rustbelt .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Mutable pointers are particularly common in multi-threaded code, and in fact, concurrent C code is notoriously difficult to analyze for memory safety bugs.
elements.sent-2411-107: The Rust compiler enforces that concurrent access or data copying across threads will never result in memory safety violations.
elements.sent-2411-108: Thus, when Rust allows relaxations to the AXM principle, runtime checks are used for memory safety.
elements.sent-2411-109: We will refer to all such references that carry dynamic checks as {\em dynamic references to avoid confusion with regular Rust references that obey the AXM principle.. Spatial Bounds Checking.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The Rust compiler aims to ensure that the accesses by owners or references to the referent are within spatial bounds.
elements.sent-2411-111: For several types of objects, the compiler can do so statically, but when it cannot, it adds runtime bounds checks.
elements.sent-2411-112: In line 6 of Figure~\ref{fig:introrust , \code{a[i] operation is checked to see if \code{i is within the boundary of \code{buf .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: If passing 3 to \code{i , the Rust code would abort the execution of the program.. Type Safety.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Rust is a type-safe language and supports safe type casting among data types using APIs provided in the standard library.
elements.sent-2411-115: All type casts guarantee that the converted values are within the legal range of the target type.
elements.sent-2411-116: Safe Rust disallows direct reinterpretation of memory to an unsafe type.
elements.sent-2411-117: For example, the conversion between pointers (\code{void\* ), non-primitives (\code{struct , \code{union , etc.) and primitives (\code{bool , \code{enum , etc.) can cause memory errors in C, but \todoadd{safe Rust forbids them.}}}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Existing Automatic C to Rust Translation
elements.sent-2411-118: There have been $2$ main approaches to automatic C-to-Rust translation: One based on compiler or static analysis, while the other using large language models (LLMs) for code synthesis.. Compiler-based Approach.
elements.sent-2411-119: Most of the work in this direction decomposes the code translation problem into two stages: (1) C to unsafe Rust, and (2) unsafe Rust to safe Rust~\cite{emre2021translating,emre2023aliasing,zhang2023ownership .}
elements.sent-2411-120: The use of \code{unsafe\{...\} keyword allows bypassing the Rust safety checks mentioned above in the enclosed code block.}
elements.sent-2411-121: The resulting Rust translation can thus rely on raw pointers (\code{mut* ), C-compatible data types, and foreign function calls to C libraries (\code{extern "C" fn ).}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The second stage of the translation is where the bulk of the challenges lie.
elements.sent-2411-123: The goal of prior automatic tools is to refactor the unsafe Rust code into safe Rust and reduce unsafe code.
elements.sent-2411-124: Laertes proposed a trial-and-error approach leveraging Rust compiler feedback to lift a certain subset of raw pointers into safer Rust references~\cite{emre2021translating .}
elements.sent-2411-125: However, a later work reports that the majority of raw pointers (79\% in their benchmarks) cannot be translated to safe references via such techniques~\cite{emre2023aliasing .}
elements.sent-2411-126: Crown proposed improvements using ownership analysis based on constraint generation and SAT-solving, for a subset of pointer types such as \code{Box<T> ~\cite{zhang2023ownership .}}
elements.sent-2411-127: We present an evaluation of these state-of-the-art tools later and find that a majority of C pointers are not lifted into safe Rust abstractions by present tools.. LLM-based Approach.
elements.sent-2411-128: Another line of work uses LLMs to translate C programs to Rust~\cite{eniser2024towards,yang2024vert .}
elements.sent-2411-129: The LLM-guided approach tends to produce code that is much more readable and idiomatic.
elements.sent-2411-130: Modern LLMs can suggest safe Rust data types, APIs, and coding conventions to use.
elements.sent-2411-131: However, LLM-based translation is often difficult to control as LLMs can make semantic mistakes~\cite{pan2024lost .}
elements.sent-2411-132: One of the most recent tools called Flourine, reports that with current LLMs (GPT-4, claude3, and so on), less than 20\% for C programs longer than 150 lines of code can be satisfactorily translated to Rust~\cite{eniser2024towards .}
elements.sent-2411-133: We evaluate Flourine as well, and we have similar findings.
elements.sent-2411-134: In summary, existing automatic techniques are insufficient to translate even small C programs of about $200$ LoC to Rust.
elements.sent-2411-135: Our observations from the user study hope to offer a holistic perspective on how users get past the inherent challenges.
elements.sent-2411-136: Prior literature has taken a bottom-up approach to the problem of C to Rust translation, aiming to show how automatic tools can address particular sub-problems encountered in translation.
elements.sent-2411-137: Our study offers a complementary perspective on how human users approach the same task.
elements.sent-2411-138: This gives us a {\em top-down view of the problem: We can see what common strategies do users use and what challenges remain thereafter.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Our participants are undergraduate students enrolled in a course on computer security.
elements.sent-2411-140: All participants are familiar with memory safety errors in C/C++ programs and were given an introduction to Rust\footnote{The content described in Section~\ref{sec:rust with example exercises constitutes the introduction given, after the participants are familiar with C memory errors.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: The task, which is part of a graded course project, is to translate $8$ real-world C programs we collected into safe Rust.
elements.sent-2411-142: Each participant is randomly assigned one out of $8$ such C programs and is asked to provide a translated program in safe Rust within 20 days\footnote{The participants conducted their work in April 2024.. .}
elements.sent-2411-143: The C programs are taken from GitHub and are small due to the 20-day time limit, with lengths varying from 322 to 536 LoC.
elements.sent-2411-144: The participants were permitted to consult the web freely and use any existing tools, for example, c2rust and LLMs.. Ethical Concerns.
elements.sent-2411-145: We followed the procedure advised by the IRB to protect the privacy of the participants and avoid bias.
elements.sent-2411-146: The participant data was anonymized and kept confidentially on our research infrastructure, without being hosted on third-party cloud services.
elements.sent-2411-147: Aggregate statistics are reported here as far as possible, and wherever code snippets are shown for illustration of a concept, they are constructed synthetically by retaining the high-level patterns seen in user submissions (not replicated).
elements.sent-2411-148: Analysis of the participants' data was conducted only after grades were finalized to avoid any influence resulting from the study on the grades.

elements.sent-2411-150: These programs are representative of C programs that implement lower-level functionality and self-manage memory where memory safety errors arise.
elements.sent-2411-151: Such functionalities are often implemented in C and used by higher-level software systems.
elements.sent-2411-152: We also considered the translation difficulty and chose programs between 300 and 600 LoC, considering the time and effort of the participants.
elements.sent-2411-153: Thus, we chose $6$ C programs\footnote{Obtained from the BSDCoreUtils~\cite{bsdcoreutil code repository (version \href{https://github.com/DiegoMagdaleno/BSDCoreUtils/tree/d2b28e08bd02da5076a876608d6431638f929849 {d2b28e0 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: All of the chosen programs (Table \ref{tab:programs ) are self-contained, requiring only the C standard library as a dependency.}
elements.sent-2411-156: The \code{expr and \code{printf are pure computation utilities for strings and numbers.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: The \code{shoco library is for data compression, and the \code{urlparser library parses URL strings.. Task Requirements.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Each individual participant is tasked to translate the assigned C program into safe Rust, satisfying two requirements, i.e., safety and functional correctness.
elements.sent-2411-159: The translated program must be written in safe Rust only.
elements.sent-2411-160: The use of keyword \code{unsafe is strictly forbidden.}
elements.sent-2411-161: For dependencies, only the Rust standard library should be used by default.
elements.sent-2411-162: When that is insufficient to implement certain functionalities, additional third-party dependencies can be used if they are well-maintained.
elements.sent-2411-163: The translated Rust program should have the equivalent external behavior to the C source program.
elements.sent-2411-164: Since most of the chosen C programs do not come with high-quality unit tests by default, we asked the participants to write their own tests with line coverage aiming for at least 85\% on the original C program to test correctness.
elements.sent-2411-165: If the source program and the translation behave the same (i.e., output, return code, effects on the file system, etc.) on those tests, we say that the translated program is \todoadd{correct if it passes all test cases created by users .}
elements.sent-2411-166: The 33 participants provided 31 {\em final translations that can compile and achieved 70\% to 98\% line coverage on the C program under their respective tests.}
elements.sent-2411-167: All of the 31 \todoadd{final translations are written in pure safe Rust, without the use of \code{unsafe .}}
elements.sent-2411-168: They form the main target of our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-169: We analyze these $31$ \todoadd{final translations to gain insights into the following research questions: RQ1.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Is there a set of common strategies that users used for successful translation? RQ2.
elements.sent-2411-171: How is the security vs. performance balance in the Rust translation? RQ3.
elements.sent-2411-172: What are the common errors and correctness gaps in translated Rust programs? RQ4.

elements.section-2411-005: Security and Performance of User Translations
elements.sent-2411-232: One of the most important motivations for translating C to Rust is to guarantee full memory safety without sacrificing performance.
elements.sent-2411-233: Some Rust safety abstractions are completely static, thereby having zero runtime costs, while others employ runtime checks.
elements.sent-2411-234: We analyze the usage of Rust abstractions in the translations obtained in our study to understand how often zero-cost safety abstractions are used.
elements.sent-2411-235: We then measure end-to-end performance of the Rust translations.
elements.sent-2411-236: These are forbidden in safe Rust, and we explain how they were translated to safe Rust code by our participants.
elements.sent-2411-237: Rust offers smart references (pointers) and safe data type abstractions which can replace raw pointers in C.
elements.sent-2411-238: We analyze (1) how often the Rust translations use those data type abstractions that encapsulate pointers, and (2) whether the memory safety properties on those types are enforced at compile time (statically) or run time (dynamically).

elements.sent-2411-275: We now turn to a comparison of performance overheads introduced when one migrates the same C code to Rust.

elements.sent-2411-291: However, there are often safe abstractions in Rust designed to achieve similar goals at low cost.
elements.sent-2411-292: We focus on two kinds of patterns present in our C programs, including mutable globals and unions, to analyze how users translate such code patterns into safe Rust.. \todoadd{Mutable Globals.}
elements.sent-2411-293: Mutable global variables in C are prone to be corrupted and exploited in real-world attacks due to their extensive lifetime across many functions and relatively predictable address~\cite{li2023hybrid,practicaldoa .}
elements.sent-2411-294: Besides being a convenient target for attackers, mutable globals are also sources of various hard-to-find temporal memory errors, and are thus discouraged by various coding guidelines~\cite{globalgoogle,globallinux,globalcpp .}
elements.sent-2411-295: Safe Rust forbids plain mutable globals because they violate the AXM principle even in single-threaded programs.
elements.sent-2411-296: Otherwise, different call frames can obtain mutable references to globals with overlapping lifetimes, leading to memory errors and incorrect optimizations in Rust.
elements.sent-2411-297: To translate C code using globals into safe Rust, we see that users devised several strategies using safe Rust abstractions, which are listed below:.
elements.sent-2411-299: A performant strategy is to identify where the global is used first and last in the program.
elements.sent-2411-300: Then, one can replace the original global variable with a local (stack or heap) object spanning the lifetime of actual use.
elements.sent-2411-301: A reference can be passed into functions that need them.
elements.sent-2411-302: When multiple mutable globals are transformed this way, they can be grouped into a single \code{struct object for better performance and to reduce code bloat.}
elements.sent-2411-303: One reference to the grouped \code{struct object with the original globals as fields at different offsets is sufficient.}
elements.sent-2411-304: This optimizes for performance as it lowers the cost of parameter passing by reference for many moved globals with similar lifetimes of use in the original C code.
elements.sent-2411-305: The fallback strategy is to create dynamic references, declared either as thread locals or true globals in Rust.
elements.sent-2411-306: For thread-local variables, single-thread synchronization (e.g., \code{RefCell ) is still needed~\cite{rusttls , but multi-thread synchronization (e.g., \code{Mutex ) is not necessary.}}}
elements.sent-2411-307: Such references incur some runtime costs.
elements.sent-2411-309: For mutable global variables that are integers, dynamic references are not needed if declaring them as \code{Atomic .}
elements.sent-2411-310: For \code{Atomic types, special APIs can mutate their values without mutable references to them.}
elements.sent-2411-311: Code examples illustrating each strategy are shown in the Appendix~\ref{sec:apdxpattern .. \todoadd{C Unions.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: Unions in C allow overlapping objects of incompatible types.
elements.sent-2411-313: However, programmers are responsible for ensuring that operations on unions are type safe.
elements.sent-2411-314: Otherwise, type safety violations can lead to both spatial and temporal memory errors~\cite{effectivesan .}
elements.sent-2411-315: In safe Rust, unions are not allowed, but various abstractions can be used to achieve similar functionality.
elements.sent-2411-316: Users translated C unions depending on different use cases:.
elements.sent-2411-318: The string compression library \code{shoco uses union to access integers as raw bytes.}
elements.sent-2411-319: Its behavior depends on the endianness of the target architecture.
elements.sent-2411-320: Some users translate such code into safe Rust API calls (\code{to(from)_le(be)_bytes ) that convert between primitives and constant-size byte arrays.}
elements.sent-2411-321: Note that the conversion is zero-cost since those APIs will be optimized away during compilation.
elements.sent-2411-322: They can also be used together with conditional compilation supported in safe Rust to match the endianness of the architecture.
elements.sent-2411-323: Please see Fig.~\ref{fig:unionpun in the Appendix~\ref{sec:apdxpattern for examples.}}
elements.sent-2411-325: Another C program \code{expr uses unions to implement variant records (sum types).}
elements.sent-2411-326: For such use cases, some users translate them into \code{enum and access them using statically-checked \code{match statements.\tododel{in safe Rust, which provides an identical abstraction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-327: Rust \code{enum is memory efficient, and the memory occupied by a Rust \code{enum depends on its largest discriminant, similar to unions in C.}}

elements.sent-2411-372: We evaluate $4$ state-of-the-art C to Rust translation tools on our C benchmarks to analyze whether their approach is effective and, when not, where the immediate gaps are.
elements.sent-2411-373: Both \laertes~and \crown~are compiler-based systems that post-process the unsafe Rust code produced by the \code{c2rust compiler~\cite{c2rust .}}
elements.sent-2411-374: Our results, shown in Table \ref{tab:existing , confirm that while both tools have the potential to produce Rust programs that compile, \todoadd{the generated code is still largely unsafe and far from safe Rust.}}
elements.sent-2411-375: We summarize their missing features next.
elements.sent-2411-376: The fraction of raw pointers that can be lifted by \laertes~and \crown~ is limited.
elements.sent-2411-377: For \laertes, Most of the data references (\todo{\unsafetyLaertesPtrDeclAvg}\% on average) are still unsafe raw pointers.
elements.sent-2411-378: For \crown, the majority of the references are also raw pointers, and safe Rust references account for less than \todo{\unsafetyCrownPtrDeclAvg}\% averaged over all the benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-380: Both of the translation tools have limited support for higher-level data types.
elements.sent-2411-381: Both \laertes and \crown are limited to three types of Rust references that are closely related to C pointers.
elements.sent-2411-382: The three types include \code{Option<\&T> (similar to \code{const T* in C), \code{Option<\&mut T> (similar to \code{T* ), and \code{Option<Box<T>> (owning \code{T* ) where \code{T is a C-compatible type.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-383: Most of the higher-level smart references that participants frequently use are out of the scopes of those tools, including \code{String , \code{\&str , \code{Vec<T> , \code{BufRead , and so on, which are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:typemap .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-385: C library calls turn into FFI calls from Rust to C in the translation of both \laertes~and \crown.
elements.sent-2411-386: C library functions generally do not have direct safe Rust mappings, and the translation is often not straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-387: Calling C functions through FFI is easy, but such handling of library calls permits unsafety.
elements.sent-2411-388: Such operations are only allowed in unsafe Rust.
elements.sent-2411-389: At the same time, C APIs often require raw pointers as parameters, which may also constrain related Rust code to operate on raw pointers.
elements.sent-2411-390: In contrast, users in our study addressed the API translation problem by utilizing safe Rust code in creative ways, as shown previously in Fig. \ref{fig:fig_api_mapping .}
elements.sent-2411-392: Aliasing references in Rust can frequently violate borrowing rules and thus can be challenging to translate.
elements.sent-2411-393: The strategies highlighted in our work, for instance in Section~\ref{sec:aliasing , are not used by these tools.}
elements.sent-2411-394: These prior tools do not aim to eliminate unsafe C code patterns such as mutable globals or unions that may be present in the \code{c2rust compiler output used as their first stage.. Memory Safety.}
elements.sent-2411-395: The end result of the code produced from prior compiler-based tools \todoadd{might be safer than C, but is not guaranteed to have full memory safety.}
elements.sent-2411-396: Recall that there are $3$ known vulnerabilities in our C benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-397: The original global array declaration in C is syntactically translated into an equivalent global array declaration in Rust.
elements.sent-2411-398: Access to such an array (rather than via a raw pointer) is bounds-checked automatically in Rust.
elements.sent-2411-399: The remaining $2$ vulnerabilities persist in the Rust translation produced by both these tools, one of which is a spatial violation and the other temporal.
elements.sent-2411-400: Raw C pointers (not integers) are involved in the unsafe code in the $2$ cases, and \laertes~and \crown~ are not yet able to lift them to safe Rust references.
elements.sent-2411-401: There are $2$ recent tools, \florine~and \toolvert, that are state-of-the-art for C to Rust translation utilizing large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-402: We report on our experience in running them on our $8$ benchmark programs.
elements.sent-2411-403: It creates two translations of the given C program, one is unsafe Rust decompiled from WASM, and the other is an LLM-\todo{generated safe Rust code.}
elements.sent-2411-404: Then, it uses fuzzing and, if needed, model-checking techniques to find tests that exhibit differences and subsequently run an automatic repair.
elements.sent-2411-405: The work on \toolvert claims that if their tool terminates normally, the resulting translation is expected to be functionally equivalent to the C code.
elements.sent-2411-406: We use ChatGPT4 as the LLM backend when evaluating \florine and \toolvert.
elements.sent-2411-407: However, neither tool generates a runnable translation for any of the C programs we consider, as shown in Table \ref{tab:existing .}
elements.sent-2411-408: This is because both tools have certain structural assumptions on the C code given to them as input.
elements.sent-2411-409: Specifically, both tools expect that the C program can be decomposed into components such that each can be independently translated and tested, since present LLMs work reasonably well on small code fragments.
elements.sent-2411-410: The decomposition of C programs into such components and merging of their Rust translations are not automated by the tools.
elements.sent-2411-411: We encountered many difficulties when trying to emulate the decomposition strategies described in their respective works.
elements.sent-2411-412: These difficulties point to a broader technical challenge that may be of independent interest for further research.. Decomposition Failures.
elements.sent-2411-413: Functions in standalone programs are connected by a call graph\tododel{that captures their invocation order .}
elements.sent-2411-414: We find it difficult to decompose such functions into independently testable modules using the strategies described in the works of \florine or \toolvert.
elements.sent-2411-415: For example, if a function $f$ calls $g$, then the component containing $f$ is required to contain $g$ since it is a dependency.
elements.sent-2411-416: This implies that on our standalone C program benchmarks, the function \code{main depends on all other functions and the component containing it \tododel{must contain all other functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-417: This includes nearly the whole program, making it too large to obtain repairable translations from LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-418: The $2$ library benchmarks are marginally better since there is no \code{main function, but the challenge persists in part here as well.}
elements.sent-2411-419: We note that the decomposition strategy proposed in \florine or \toolvert can produce multiple translations of the same function that are inconsistent and cannot be merged into one.
elements.sent-2411-420: Say we have two components, one containing function $\{f,g\}$ and another containing function $\{h,g\}$.
elements.sent-2411-421: The two components are translated independently and thus multiple Rust translations of the function $g$ are obtained.
elements.sent-2411-422: We often find that these translations have conflicting type definitions and incompatible types that are difficult to merge into a single translation\tododel{ for $g$}.
elements.sent-2411-423: We are not aware of better ways to decompose our C programs in components small enough to feed to \florine or \toolvert.
elements.sent-2411-424: We fed the whole program to these tools and the translated Rust code does not compile.
elements.sent-2411-425: We then experimented with several versions manually to best split each function in a separate component, while including only a minimal number of dependencies, such that the size of component is small enough to work with LLMs.

elements.sent-2411-427: Existing tools share a common challenge in devising workable decomposition strategies for long C code.. \end{tcolorbox.}

elements.sent-2411-481: Our analysis of user-provided translations highlights several points where automatic translation strategies deviate from those taken by human users.
elements.sent-2411-482: We reflect on why there is such a gap and summarize takeaways for automatic translation.
elements.sent-2411-484: Our observation is that there are many choices to be considered when translating a C program to Rust, i.e., there is no one-size-fits-all strategy to take.
elements.sent-2411-485: The resulting translations, while satisfying memory safety, can have varying levels of functional correctness, performance, and grace in handling runtime safety exceptions.
elements.sent-2411-486: The balance between them is a matter of {\em policy .}
elements.sent-2411-487: Full functional equivalence can require significant effort and may even go against the purpose of code migration~\cite{verhoef2000realities .}
elements.sent-2411-488: To characterize what behaviors must be kept and what behaviors can change in the translation, we may need more thorough unit tests or other forms of specifications.
elements.sent-2411-489: Another example of an important policy decision is about the data types and APIs to use, which can lead to multiple ways of translating the same program\tododel{.}
elements.sent-2411-490: Different data types, APIs, or third-party dependencies can be used to implement the same functionality with different trade-offs in performance, memory overhead, compatibility, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-491: In summary, users may want explicit control over policy decisions even when using automatic tools.
elements.sent-2411-493: Once policy decisions are clear, automatic mechanisms can enable search for translations.
elements.sent-2411-494: Our observation is that there are several immediate and open problems that, if addressed, can make C to Rust code automatic translator much more usable.
elements.sent-2411-495: So far, few of the previous work explicitly models data types and APIs in the Rust standard library, such as the ones summarized in Fig.~\ref{fig:typemap .}
elements.sent-2411-496: We believe it is essential to overcome the language differences between C and Rust and move away from unsafe code.. (2) Mergeable Decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-497: Finding the right way to decompose programs into smaller components is challenging but appears necessary for LLMs with limited context windows.
elements.sent-2411-498: At the same time, decomposition need not be one-shot.
elements.sent-2411-499: It is useful to explore if it is possible to incrementally transform identified components, such that a partial Rust translation can be used alongside the untranslated part of C.
elements.sent-2411-500: Some users reported in their (optional) qualitative feedback about their experience.
elements.sent-2411-501: Based on our findings, we foresee $3$ program analyses as immediately useful: \todoadd{(1) Refactoring Global Variables Through Lifetime and Def-Use Analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-502: Use of mutable globals differentiates C and safe Rust programs.
elements.sent-2411-503: As observed in our user study, many globals are accessed within a thread and can be moved to the heap or stack, possibly in grouped \code{struct -typed objects for efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-504: Precise analysis of lifetime of globals is a promising next step.
elements.sent-2411-505: Data type analysis directly impacts how references are lifted to Rust.
elements.sent-2411-506: Code blocks and pointers in C can often be elided after type lifting, which is observed from both translations by our users and in our extensibility analysis on public code(e.g., Linked list pointer manipulations to \code{Vec API calls).}
elements.sent-2411-507: LLMs can be useful for pattern recognition tasks, according to qualitative feedback received from our users, and it would be promising to use them for type mapping suggestions of C objects to Rust.. (3) Lifting Unions.

elements.subsection-2411-012: Examples of Logical Translation Errors by Users
elements.sent-2411-586: Here we describe several logical translation errors we observed in translations of fmt program, along with a possible fix for each.
elements.sent-2411-587: Surprisingly, the bugs are in code lines covered by passing tests.
elements.sent-2411-588: We have two failing tests demonstrating each of the translation bugs in Fig. \ref{fig:exp_diff0 .}
elements.sent-2411-589: Another bug is revealed when there are odd numbers rather than even numbers of padding spaces.
elements.sent-2411-590: The unit tests written by the user miss those cases.
elements.sent-2411-591: The first discrepancy is due to calling the wrong API when computing the display width of the characters.
elements.sent-2411-592: The translation used \code{char::len_utf8 which computed the size in bytes of a Unicode character instead of display width.}
elements.sent-2411-593: For the frequent ASCII characters, the size in bytes happens to be 1, which coincides with their display size.
elements.sent-2411-594: However, the failing input "\code{z u00df u6c34 U0001d10b " is an example where the size in bytes is different from the display width.}
elements.sent-2411-595: It contains 4 valid Unicode characters, each occupying 1, 2, 3, and 4 bytes respectively.
elements.sent-2411-596: But the total display width of the input string is 5 bytes, not equivalent to the total size in bytes.
elements.sent-2411-597: It does not get revealed by the passing unit test where characters are all simple ASCII characters.
elements.sent-2411-598: The fix is to use the \code{c.width method, shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:exp_diff0 .}}
elements.sent-2411-599: The second discrepancy is due to an off-by-one error in the computation of padding spaces, making the Rust translation correct only when the number of spaces is even.
elements.sent-2411-600: The Rust program uses a slightly different computation to split the padding spaces into leading and trailing ones, compared to the C program.
elements.sent-2411-601: While the C program behaves like a round-up division, the Rust program takes the floor division.
elements.sent-2411-602: The passing test does not reveal this difference.
elements.sent-2411-603: The correct fix is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:exp_diff0 .}

elements.sent-2411-607: All users assigned this program omit this functionality in their translation.
elements.sent-2411-608: With a deeper look, it turns out that such behavior is not easily implementable when using \code{String type to represent \code{p1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-609: In Rust, characters stored in a \code{String data type are limited to Unicode characters rather than arbitrary raw bytes.}
elements.sent-2411-610: When they use the Rust API \code{read_line to read a \code{String from the stream, it either returns a \code{String instance or \code{Err result if there are any invalid characters.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-611: When it returns an \code{Err , there is no API to tell where are the invalid characters.}
elements.sent-2411-612: With a more careful search, we find an API \code{from_utf8_lossy that is closer to what we want.}
elements.sent-2411-613: This API can create a \code{String from a buffer with each invalid character replaced with a single Unicode \code{? .}}
elements.sent-2411-614: We soon realize that this API also does not bridge the semantic gap.
elements.sent-2411-615: The C code not only outputs \code{? at the position of invalid characters, but also outputs the same number of \code{? as the number of invalid bytes, which can vary.}}
elements.sent-2411-616: The \code{from_utf8_lossy API is not behaving the same way.}
elements.sent-2411-617: In summary, we cannot find APIs on \code{String to preserve the same semantics when using \code{String to represent a \code{p1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-618: As a result, we can consider refining the choice of Rust data types for \code{p1 .}
elements.sent-2411-619: The type of \code{p1 should carry sufficient information and support APIs to identify (1) the locations of invalid characters, and (2) the number of invalid bytes at each location.}
elements.sent-2411-620: Many Rust data types can represent a string but not all of them can meet our requirements.
elements.sent-2411-621: One example is to consider using types \code{Vec<io::Result<char>> to represent a line.}
elements.sent-2411-622: It is roughly a vector of Unicode chars, and invalid chars inside it can be represented by a special character.
elements.sent-2411-623: Another possible choice is \code{std::ffi::OsString , which is a platform-native string type.}
elements.sent-2411-624: It supports holding invalid UTF-8 characters.
elements.sent-2411-625: Here we use \code{Vec<u8> as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-626: Now the question is about how to use \code{Vec<u8> to achieve the intended program behavior, i.e., to parse Unicode characters and replace invalid ones with the correct number of question marks.}
elements.sent-2411-627: One option is to use \code{std::str::Utf8Chunks structure from the Rust standard library.}
elements.sent-2411-628: This structure allows iteratively check \code{u8 bytes referred by an immutable slice and convert them to Unicode characters if valid.}
elements.sent-2411-629: Fig.~\ref{fig:exp_lifting_fix shows the fixed version of Translation A with the correct Unicode character parsing logic when using \code{Vec<u8> type for \code{p1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-630: Because of this new choice of type, the original code to read lines and parse characters needs to be updated accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-631: We update multiple lines in the method calls on \code{stream to read the input and iteratively save the line-separated input into a \code{Vec<u8> .}}
elements.sent-2411-632: The eventually fixed translation (shown in Fig. \ref{fig:exp_lifting_fix ) can pass the differing test mentioned in Fig. \ref{fig:exp_diff0 .}}
elements.sent-2411-633: The average overhead is around 13\% across the eight Rust translations.
